Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after my other commitments, but it rarely stuck to a schedule. I'd usually end up doing a couple hours here and there, maybe reading through materials or working on whatever was due soon. I aimed for consistency but boredom would set in and I'd find myself drifting to other things. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a bit with focus, but motivation was always a struggle.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd tell myself I'd study regularly each week, but in reality, I'd slack off when nothing was immediately due and then cram when assignments were coming up. Some weeks I barely touched the course material, other weeks I'd put in serious hours. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I managed to get through with everything else going on.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or just checking if there was something new posted. Sometimes I'd log in out of guilt, like I knew I should be doing something productive even if I didn't feel like it. Notifications on my phone would prompt me too. It wasn't really excitement that drove me there, more like obligation or anxiety about falling behind.
Q4: There were a few weeks mid-semester where logging in became somewhat automatic, usually in the morning with my coffee. I'd check announcements and skim through new materials. But that routine faded when things got stressful and I started feeling more detached from the course. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me establish a bit of a habit for a while, but my low motivation made it hard to sustain.
Q5: The written materials and lecture notes were the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and highlight key sections, then make my own summaries. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when I was confused about something and could see how others approached it. Videos were okay but I found myself zoning out during them, so text-based resources worked better for my style.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary videos and some of the optional readings. They felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. The peer interaction features I used minimally because I didn't feel connected to other students and it seemed like more effort than it was worth. When you're already bored with the core material, optional content just feels like a burden.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd engage with it. Clear instructions and relevance to assessments made a big difference. Anything that felt vague or disconnected from what I'd be evaluated on just didn't seem worth the effort, especially when motivation was already low.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, while required ones had that weight of consequences attached. With required stuff, there's anxiety about failing, so I'd push through even when bored. Optional things? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The stress of mandatory work was sometimes the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and PDFs, then study from my laptop without being connected to the LMS. I'd make handwritten notes and summaries, which is how I really process information. Sometimes I'd use external websites to clarify concepts that the course materials didn't explain well enough. Most of that wouldn't show up in any system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my printed notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also discuss concepts with a friend from another course who understood some of the topics better than me. Those conversations were genuinely helpful for my learning, but obviously there's no record of that anywhere in the system.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a reading or lecture slides once, download them, and then never open them again on the LMS. All my subsequent studying would be from the saved files on my computer. It was just more convenient and I could study without needing internet. The LMS would show one view, but I might have spent hours with that same material offline.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was stressed about multiple courses, feeling fatigued, and the boredom with this particular course peaked. The material felt repetitive and I couldn't muster enthusiasm. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up, I just shut down a bit. The mindfulness exercises helped manage the anxiety somewhat, but they couldn't fix the underlying lack of interest.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had competing deadlines from other courses, this one often got pushed aside. Fatigue was a big factor too—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to engage with material that already bored me. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid them rather than seek help, which probably hurt my assignment completion rate. It was a cycle of avoidance.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd watch a video while thinking about other things, or read a page three times without absorbing anything. The activity was there but meaningful engagement wasn't. It was more about going through the motions than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The boredom made it easy to justify waiting until later. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure, but really I was just avoiding something that didn't interest me. Fear of doing poorly also played a role—if I didn't try hard, I couldn't really fail, you know? It's not logical but that's how my brain worked.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. My LMS usage would spike right before something was due. I'd cram reading and rush through assignments in the final hours. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. They created stress but also structure, which I apparently needed.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to improve, but other times it just added to my stress and made me want to avoid the course more. The delay between submission and feedback meant I'd often moved on mentally by the time I received it, so it didn't always influence my next steps.
Q18: The course structure was fairly standard—weekly modules with readings and assignments. Having clear weekly expectations helped me know what I should be doing, even if I didn't always do it. But the structure also felt monotonous, which fed into my boredom. More variety or interactive elements might have kept me more engaged throughout the semester.
Q19: The heavy reliance on text-based materials suited my learning style, which was good. But the lack of immediate feedback on smaller tasks made it easy to fall behind without realizing it. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't create genuine engagement. Having all materials accessible was convenient, but it also meant I could always tell myself I'd do it later.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reading everything, watching videos, checking forums, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that affects their ability to complete work even though they're attempting to stay connected. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That actually sounds somewhat like me. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in the LMS, and I managed to pass despite moderate system activity. Some people are efficient—they know what they need, grab it, and study elsewhere. The LMS is just a portal to materials for some of us, not where the actual learning happens. Performance can be disconnected from logged activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you click on, how long you're logged in. They're good for tracking deadline-driven behavior and general engagement trends. But they miss the quality of attention, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood anything. They capture presence but not comprehension or genuine engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being curious, thinking critically, wanting to do well because the content matters to you. For me, true engagement was rare this semester. I was present but not really invested most of the time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored I was, or how much I struggled with motivation despite trying mindfulness techniques. It wouldn't capture the offline studying, the conversations with friends, or the mental energy I spent just trying to make myself care. It also wouldn't show that passing this course was more about survival than genuine learning. The human experience behind the data is completely invisible.
